sources.title: Pearson Edexcel A Level Further Mathematics 9FM0 specification

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to understand mathematics and mathematical processes in ways that promote confidence, foster enjoyment and provide a strong foundation for progress to further study.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r1-b0-l0,p10-r1-b0-l1, aim-1

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to recognise when mathematics can be used to analyse and solve a problem in context.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r19-b0-l0, aim-10

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to represent situations mathematically and understand the relationship between problems in context and mathematical models that may be applied to solve them.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r21-b0-l0,p10-r21-b0-l1, aim-11

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to draw diagrams and sketch graphs to help explore mathematical situations and interpret solutions.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r23-b0-l0,p10-r23-b0-l1, aim-12

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to make deductions and inferences and draw conclusions by using mathematical reasoning.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r25-b0-l0, aim-13

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to interpret solutions and communicate their interpretation effectively in the context of the problem.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r27-b0-l0,p10-r27-b0-l1, aim-14

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to read and comprehend mathematical arguments, including justifications of methods and formulae, and communicate their understanding.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r29-b0-l0,p10-r29-b0-l1, aim-15

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to read and comprehend articles concerning applications of mathematics and communicate their understanding.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r31-b0-l0,p10-r31-b0-l1, aim-16

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to use technology such as calculators and computers effectively, and recognise when such use may be inappropriate.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r33-b0-l0,p10-r33-b0-l1, aim-17

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to take increasing responsibility for their own learning and the evaluation of their own mathematical development.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r35-b0-l0,p10-r35-b0-l1, aim-18

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to extend their range of mathematical skills and techniques.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r3-b0-l0, aim-2

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to understand coherence and progression in mathematics and how different areas of mathematics are connected.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r5-b0-l0,p10-r5-b0-l1, aim-3

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to apply mathematics in other fields of study and be aware of the relevance of mathematics to the world of work and to situations in society in general.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r7-b0-l0,p10-r7-b0-l1, aim-4

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to use their mathematical knowledge to make logical and reasoned decisions in solving problems both within pure mathematics and in a variety of contexts, and communicate the mathematical rationale for these decisions clearly.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r9-b0-l0,p10-r9-b0-l1,p10-r9-b0-l2, aim-5

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to reason logically and recognise incorrect reasoning.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r11-b0-l0, aim-6

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to generalise mathematically.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r13-b0-l0, aim-7

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to construct mathematical proofs.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r15-b0-l0, aim-8

statements.statementText: This qualification aims to enable students to use their mathematical skills and techniques to solve challenging problems which require them to decide on the solution strategy.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 10, line IDs p10-r0-b2-l0,p10-r17-b0-l0,p10-r17-b0-l1, aim-9

statements.statementText: Construct proofs using mathematical induction. Contexts include sums of series, divisibility and powers of matrices.
notesText: Include induction proofs for summation of series, for example Σ from r = 1 to n of r³ = ¼n²(n + 1)²; divisibility, for example 3²ⁿ + 11 is divisible by 4; and powers of matrices.

statements.topicHeading: 2.1 Solve quadratic equations with real coefficients
statements.statementText: Solve any quadratic equation with real coefficients.
statements.sourceLocator: Official specification PDF page 13, line IDs p13-r28-b0-l0,p13-r28-b0-l1,p13-r28-b0-l2,p13-r28-b0-l3, OFFICIAL-Edexcel-9FM0-CP-2.1

conceptLinks.evidenceSpan: Solve any quadratic equation

statements.topicHeading: 2.1 Solve cubic and quartic equations with real coefficients
statements.statementText: Solve cubic or quartic equations with real coefficients.
notesText: Given sufficient information to deduce at least one root for a cubic, or at least one complex root or quadratic factor for a quartic, solve the equation completely.
examplesText: f(z) = 2z³ + 5z² + 7z + 10, given that z + 2 is a factor; g(x) = x⁴ − x³ + 6x² + 14x − 20, given g(1) = 0 and g(−2) = 0.

statements.statementText: Multiply and divide complex numbers in modulus argument form.
notesText: Knowledge of the results: z₁ z₂ = z₁ z₂, z₁/z₂ = z₁/z₂, arg(z₁ z₂) = arg z₁ + arg z₂, arg((z₁)/(z₂)) = arg z₁ − arg z₂. Knowledge of radians and compound angle formulae is assumed.

statements.statementText: Construct and interpret simple loci in the argand diagram such as |z − a| > r and arg(z − a) = θ.
notesText: To include loci such as |z − a| = b, |z − a| = |z − b|, arg(z − a) = β, and regions such as |z − a| ≤ |z − b|, |z − a| ≤ b, α < arg(z − a) < β. Knowledge of radians is assumed.

statements.statementText: Understand de Moivre’s theorem and use it to find multiple angle formulae and sums of series.
notesText: Use z + 1/z = 2 cos θ and z − 1/z = 2i sin θ to express multiple angles and powers of trigonometric functions, and to sum series.
examplesText: 1 + z + z² + … + zⁿ⁻¹ = 1 + i cot(π/(2n)), where z = cos(π/n) + i sin(π/n) and n is a positive integer.

statements.statementText: Know and use eⁱθ = cos θ + i sin θ and the form z = reⁱθ.
notesText: Use cos θ = (eⁱθ + e⁻ⁱθ)/2 and sin θ = (eⁱθ − e⁻ⁱθ)/(2i).

statements.statementText: Use matrices to represent linear transformations in 2-D.

statements.statementText: The general ideas of algorithms and the implementation of an algorithm given by a flow chart or text.
notesText: The meaning of the order of an algorithm is expected. Students will be expected to determine the order of a given algorithm and the order of standard network problems.

The efficiency of an algorithm is a measure of the ‘run-time’ of the algorithm and in most cases is proportional to the number of operations that must be carried out.
The size of a problem is a measure of its complexity and so in the case of algorithms on graphs it is likely to be the number of vertices on the graph.
The order of an algorithm is a measure of its efficiency as a function of the size of the problem.
